Title: Ustilago maydis Nit2 Regulates Nitrate Utilisation During Biotrophy and Affects Amino Acid Metabolism of Galls Under Nitrogen Depletion

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: FB1 × FB2 wild-type background; Δnit2 mutants in FB1 and FB2
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: nitrate; amino acid composition of galls
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Nit2-dependent expression of the nitrate transporter is strongly reduced in Δnit2 galls, especially under host nitrogen depletion, consistent with impaired nitrate utilization and nitrate accumulation in galls.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. nrt is part of the nitrate assimilation cluster required for nitrate utilisation in U. maydis. 2. RNA-seq showed nrt transcripts were almost 30-fold lower in Δnit2 galls under −N and 8-fold lower in 1N. 3. The paper links loss of Nit2-dependent nrt expression with nitrate accumulation in Δnit2 galls under nitrogen depletion.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR validation of cluster genes; gall metabolite profiling
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced during biotrophy in wild type, especially under host nitrogen depletion; reduced in Δnit2 galls
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nitrate serves as an inorganic nitrogen source for fungal assimilation during biotrophy
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Biotrophy in maize leaf galls at 8 dpi under 1N versus nitrogen-depleted (−N) host fertilization
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although functionally central to nitrate uptake, nrt itself was not directly manipulated in this study; evidence is from Nit2-dependent expression during biotrophy.

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: FB1 × FB2 wild-type background; Δnit2 mutants in FB1 and FB2
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: asparagine; glutamine; GABA; amino acid pool
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression of the amino acid permease UMAG_00056 is reduced in Δnit2 galls, suggesting Nit2 contributes to amino acid uptake remodeling during biotrophy.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UMAG_00056 transcript abundance was reduced about 3-fold in Δnit2 galls by RT-qPCR. 2. It remained more than 2-fold induced in Δnit2 under −N compared with 1N, indicating additional regulators besides Nit2. 3. The gene is among the more abundant amino acid transporter transcripts in galls and may contribute to compensation for impaired nitrate use.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: amino acid permease / amino acid transporter
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed during biotrophy; fourth/fifth most abundant amino acid transporter transcript in wild-type galls; induced under −N
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Amino acids function as major organic nitrogen sources during fungal biotrophy
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Biotrophy in maize leaf galls at 8 dpi under 1N versus −N
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The specific transported amino acid substrate was not assigned in this study.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: FB1 × FB2 wild-type background; Δnit2 mutants in FB1 and FB2
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: nitrate assimilation affecting amino acid metabolism in galls
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces nitrate to nitrite during fungal nitrate assimilation.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies assimilatory inorganic nitrogen for downstream amino acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step not established; expression is strongly Nit2-dependent during biotrophy
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: nar1 expression is strongly dependent on Nit2 during biotrophy, and loss of this regulation is associated with impaired nitrate utilization and nitrate accumulation in Δnit2 galls under nitrogen depletion.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. nar1 transcripts were almost 10-fold reduced in Δnit2 galls by RNA-seq under −N. 2. RT-qPCR confirmed strong induction in wild-type galls under host N depletion and drastic repression in Δnit2 galls, including ~93-fold reduction under −N. 3. Absence of N-dependent induction in Δnit2 indicates nitrate utilisation is hampered in mutant galls. 4. Despite this, pathogenicity was unaffected, showing nitrate assimilation is dispensable for virulence during biotrophy.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; gall nitrate metabolite profiling; Δnit2 mutant analysis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced in wild-type galls under host nitrogen depletion; near detection limit in Δnit2 galls
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Assimilated nitrate supports fungal nitrogen metabolism and amino acid production
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Biotrophy in maize leaf galls at 8 dpi under 1N versus −N
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: nar1 belongs to a nitrate assimilation cluster with nrt and nir1; mutants in structural genes are reported to have reduced virulence in other backgrounds, but this study focused on Nit2 regulation rather than nar1 knockout.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: FB1 × FB2 wild-type background; Δnit2 mutants in FB1 and FB2
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: nitrate assimilation affecting amino acid metabolism in galls
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces nitrite to ammonium in assimilatory nitrate reduction.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies assimilatory ammonium for downstream amino acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step not established; expression is strongly Nit2-dependent during biotrophy
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: nir1 expression is almost abolished in Δnit2 galls, demonstrating that nitrate utilization during biotrophy depends strongly on Nit2.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RNA-seq showed nir1 transcripts were reduced more than 30-fold in Δnit2 galls in both N regimes. 2. RT-qPCR confirmed wild-type induction under host N depletion and extremely strong repression in Δnit2 galls, including >50-fold reduction under −N and transcript levels near detection limit. 3. The loss of N-dependent induction in Δnit2 indicates nitrate assimilation is broadly blocked during biotrophy.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced in wild-type galls under host nitrogen depletion; near detection limit in Δnit2 galls
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Assimilatory ammonium derived from nitrite feeds amino acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Biotrophy in maize leaf galls at 8 dpi under 1N versus −N
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: nir1 showed the strongest Nit2-dependent repression among tested nitrate assimilation genes.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: FB1 × FB2 wild-type background; Δnit2 mutants in FB1 and FB2
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: glutamate; glutamine/asparagine-related amino acid metabolism in galls
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes reductive amination of 2-oxoglutarate to glutamate using NADPH.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies central amino donor pool for downstream amino acid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: UMAG_02801 transcript abundance is reduced about 10-fold in Δnit2 galls, linking Nit2 to glutamate-centered nitrogen metabolism during biotrophy.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RT-qPCR confirmed that NADP glutamate dehydrogenase transcripts were about 10-fold lower in Δnit2 galls in both N regimes. 2. The gene appeared slightly transcriptionally repressed in wild-type galls under −N relative to 1N. 3. Its Nit2 dependence indicates Nit2 affects organic nitrogen metabolism beyond the nitrate assimilation cluster.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed during biotrophy in galls; lower in Δnit2 under both N regimes
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Glutamate is a central amino donor and precursor for GABA, proline, glutamine and related nitrogen metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Biotrophy in maize leaf galls at 8 dpi under 1N versus −N
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study measured transcript effects only; no enzyme activity assay was reported.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: FB1 × FB2 wild-type background; Δnit2 mutants in FB1 and FB2
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: nitrate utilization; amino acid metabolism in maize leaf galls
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: transcriptional activation of nitrogen utilization and transport genes during biotrophy
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: nir1 (UMAG_11104); nar1 (UMAG_03847); nrt (UMAG_11105); UMAG_02801; UMAG_00056; dur3-3 (UMAG_04577); ump2 (UMAG_05889); additional nitrogen metabolism and transport genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Strongly activates the nitrate assimilation cluster during biotrophy; partially activates selected amino acid transport and nitrogen metabolism genes
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: nitrate utilization; nitrogen source adaptation; amino acid uptake/metabolism during biotrophy
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major regulator of nitrate assimilation during biotrophy; decisive for induction of nar1/nir1/nrt and thereby fungal nitrate use, but not essential for virulence
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Nit2 is required for induction of the nitrate assimilation cluster during biotrophy and affects gall amino acid composition under nitrogen depletion, causing nitrate accumulation and reduced asparagine/glutamine/GABA in Δnit2 galls, while pathogenicity remains unchanged.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Δnit2 mutants in the FB1 × FB2 background cannot efficiently utilize non-favored nitrogen sources and show delayed filamentation, but normal pathogenicity in maize. 2. RNA-seq revealed that about one third of Nit2-dependent genes during biotrophy are linked to nitrogen metabolism/transport, with only minor overlap to saprotrophic sporidia. 3. Induction of the nitrate assimilation cluster, especially nar1, nir1 and nrt, is essentially dependent on Nit2 during biotrophy. 4. Under host nitrogen depletion, Δnit2 galls accumulate nitrate and show lower asparagine, glutamine and GABA, indicating altered gall amino acid metabolism. 5. Total protein content and virulence are not reduced, showing fungal nitrate use is dispensable during biotrophy and can likely be compensated by organic nitrogen sources.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nitrogen assimilation supports fungal growth and amino acid metabolism during host colonization
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed during biotrophy; only modestly induced (~1.6-fold) in wild-type galls under −N versus 1N
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Active during biotrophy in maize leaf galls at 8 dpi
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Nitrogen availability; release from nitrogen catabolite repression; host nitrogen depletion
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: native promoter in wild type; gene deletion in Δnit2 mutants
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes delayed filamentation and major repression of nitrate assimilation genes, but no detectable reduction in gall pathogenicity or protein content in this background
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Biotrophy in maize fourth-leaf galls at 8 dpi under regular nitrogen (1N) or host nitrogen depletion (−N)
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: gene knockout in FB1 and FB2; RT-qPCR; RNA-seq; gall metabolite profiling; pathogenicity assays; fungal biomass quantification
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Nit2 regulates a different but partially overlapping gene set during biotrophy versus saprotrophic sporidia. The source species is the fungus, while the metabolic phenotype was measured in infected maize galls.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential antifungal target for disrupting nitrate assimilation and nitrogen-source adaptation, but limited value alone because loss of Nit2 does not reduce virulence in the tested natural mating background.
